name: Border between Castilla y León by Redecilla del Camino — circular from Castildelgado
name_original: Límite entre Castilla y León por Redecilla del Camino — circular desde Castildelgado
region_intro: Jogging around Castildelgado offers access to diverse landscapes, from the mountainous terrain of the Sierra de La Demanda to the gentler rolling hills and river paths of the "Riojilla Burgalesa" region. The area is characterized by a blend of accessible trails and more challenging routes, providing varied experiences for runners. Its location on the historic French Way of Saint James also provides well-established paths connecting to neighboring villages. These routes often feature a mix of natural scenery and historical elements. Best jogging routes around Castildelgado The most popular jogging route is Redecilla del Camino - Camino Francés Loop, a 4.5 miles (7.2 km) trail that takes 46 minutes to complete. This route follows a section of the historic Camino Francés, offering a blend of cultural and natural scenery. Another top favourite among local runners is Border between Castilla y León by Redecilla del Camino — circular from Castildelgado, a moderate 8.9 miles (14.3 km) path. This circular route provides varied terrain and views as it traverses the border region. Local runners also love the Leiva Castle loop from Herramélluri, a 4.8 miles (7.8 km) trail leading through historical areas, often completed in about 49 minutes. Jogging around Castildelgado is defined by its proximity to the Sierra de La Demanda, the gentle river paths of the "Riojilla Burgalesa," and sections of the Camino de Santiago. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate to difficult routes. The routes in Castildelgado are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from 1 review. More than 50 runners have used komoot to explore Castildelgado's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many running routes are available around Castildelgado?
A: There are nearly 20 jogging routes around Castildelgado, offering a variety of experiences. These include paths through the mountainous Sierra de La Demanda, gentler river paths in the "Riojilla Burgalesa" region, and sections of the historic French Way of Saint James.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on jogging routes near Castildelgado?
A: The terrain around Castildelgado is quite diverse. You can find challenging, elevated paths in the mountainous Sierra de La Demanda, offering forests and panoramic views. Alternatively, the "Riojilla Burgalesa" region provides gentler, rolling hills and river paths, ideal for more relaxed runs. Many routes also follow the well-trodden paths of the Camino de Santiago.

Q: Are there running routes suitable for beginners or those looking for an easy run?
A: While there are no routes specifically classified as 'easy' in our guide, many of the 15 moderate routes can be enjoyed by beginners or those seeking a less strenuous run. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20151098" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Running loop from Belorado&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 5.4 km path with minimal elevation gain, offering a pleasant experience.

Q: What about more challenging or long-distance running trails?
A: For experienced runners seeking a challenge, Castildelgado offers several difficult routes. A notable option is &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38168866" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;The Sampol Little Airplane – Torreon Sor Maria de Leiva loop from Grañón&lt;/a&gt;, which spans over 26 km with significant elevation changes, providing a rigorous workout and expansive views.

Q: Are there any circular running routes in the area?
A: Yes, there are several circular running routes available. A popular choice is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1050010480/border-between-castilla-y-leon-by-redecilla-del-camino-circular-from-castildelgado" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Border between Castilla y León by Redecilla del Camino — circular from Castildelgado&lt;/a&gt;, a moderate 14.3 km path that allows you to start and finish in the same location while enjoying varied scenery.

Q: Can I combine jogging with exploring historical sites?
A: Absolutely! Castildelgado is located on the historic French Way of Saint James, meaning many running paths follow this ancient pilgrimage route. You can jog through traditional villages and past historical elements like the 16th-century Casa de Gil Delgado or the Parish Church of San Pedro. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31850227" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Leiva Castle loop from Herramélluri&lt;/a&gt; is another great option, leading through historical areas.

Q: Are there any interesting landmarks or attractions along the running routes?
A: Yes, several routes offer glimpses of historical landmarks. For instance, the area features sites like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5557168" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Leiva Castle&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5135703" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Boundary Between La Rioja and Castilla y León on the Camino de Santiago&lt;/a&gt;. You might also encounter charming settlements like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4705973" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Belorado&lt;/a&gt; with its &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4631984" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Plaza Mayor&lt;/a&gt;, or the historic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/655221" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Santo Domingo de la Calzada Bridge&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What do other runners enjoy most about jogging in Castildelgado?
A: The running routes in Castildelgado are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 5.0 stars. Runners often praise the blend of natural scenery and historical elements, especially the sections of the Camino de Santiago, and the variety of terrain available, from gentle paths to more challenging mountain trails.

Q: Are the running trails around Castildelgado suitable for families?
A: Many of the moderate routes, particularly those along the gentler river paths or shorter sections of the Camino de Santiago, can be suitable for families. These paths often offer a good balance of natural beauty and manageable distances, allowing for an enjoyable outing for all ages. Always check the route's distance and elevation profile to ensure it matches your family's fitness level.

Q: Are there dog-friendly running trails near Castildelgado?
A: The natural landscapes around Castildelgado, including the Sierra de La Demanda and the numerous trails, are generally welcoming for dogs. Many paths, especially those away from urban centers, offer ample space for dogs to run alongside you. However, it's always advisable to keep your dog on a leash, especially near livestock or in protected natural areas, and to carry water for both yourself and your pet.

Q: Is public transport available to access the running routes?
A: While Castildelgado is a small village, its location on the Camino de Santiago means it is connected to a network of paths that can be accessed from various points. For specific public transport options to trailheads, it's best to check local bus schedules connecting Castildelgado to larger towns in the Burgos province, which may offer more frequent services.

Q: Where can I park my car when going for a run in Castildelgado?
A: As a small village, Castildelgado typically offers street parking or small public parking areas, especially near the village center or close to the Camino de Santiago access points. If you plan to start a route from a specific trailhead outside the village, look for designated parking spots or safe roadside parking, ensuring you do not obstruct local traffic or private property.
tips: Beautiful church built in the 12th century and rebuilt in the 17th and 18th centuries. Its tower is square in brick and has a portico with a high Renaissance arcade presided over by a stone image of the Virgen de la Calle, from the 14th century. Inside there is a Romanesque baptismal font from the 12th century.

The kingdoms of Castilla and León have a remote origin, sometimes separated, sometimes united, either by Muslim domination or by different personal interests of the rulers. Castilla-León is currently one of the 17 autonomous regions of Spain.
